topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit me first, a familiar comfort that suddenly curdled into something sharp and wrong. I’d been walking the perimeter of the old orchard, a habit born of a deep-seated need to know what was happening in this quiet corner of the town. My eyes scanned the line of saplings I’d nurtured when I was ten—the ones I’d painstakingly watered and protected. Then I saw it. Where a sturdy oak had stood, now there was only a ragged, freshly cut stump, the earth around it disturbed like a recent excavation. A cold knot tightened in my chest, and I stopped dead. I circled the debris slowly, my footsteps unnaturally loud on the gravel path. "Hello?" I called out, my voice sounding thin and brittle in the stillness. No answer. Just the rustle of dry leaves in the breeze, mocking my intrusion.

[story 2]

I always check the boundaries. It’s just a routine, a way to keep the edges of my world defined and safe. Today, however, the routine fractured. I was inspecting the edge of the woods behind my property, the sun glinting off the dew on the ferns. That’s when I noticed the gaping void where the towering maple used to be. It wasn't just missing; it was gone, reduced to splintered wood and bare soil. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. I crouched down, running a hand over the raw cut where the trunk had been. The wood felt alien, wrong. My jaw clenched so hard my teeth ached. I stood up abruptly, scanning the dense undergrowth, every shadow seeming to hold a hidden shape. I didn't move for a long moment, just absorbing the emptiness, my gaze darting from the wound to the surrounding trees, searching for a sign, a trace of who had made this change.

[story 3]

It started as a flicker of unease, a prickling sensation on the back of my neck that wouldn't subside. I’ve learned to read the subtle shifts in the environment—the way the light falls at dusk, the sound of a branch snapping in the wind. But this was different. I found myself tracing the line of the old border, my pace quickening from a measured stroll to a near-stride. Then I saw the devastation. The young birch I’d planted years ago, the one I’d watched grow into a beacon of summer shade, was gone. Just stumps, raw and ugly, marking where life had been. A wave of icy certainty washed over me. I stood frozen, my hands balling into tight fists at my sides. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm against the silence. I forced myself to take a slow, deliberate breath, my eyes sweeping the horizon, cataloging every movement, every potential threat lurking just beyond the reach of my sight.
